context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i’m kinda lost on this climate action plan thing tbh. can u just explain what i’m supposed to do from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I explain anything, tell me: have you picked a case study city yet, and do you have your SWOT Analysis done? That'll help me know exactly where you're starting from.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment from scratch. Rather than walking them through everything, I should ask a targeted question to assess what they already know and where specifically they feel lost. This avoids over-scaffolding and gets them engaged in the thinking process right away.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but i’m still confused ngl. i have a city i think, but can u just explain the whole assignment first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but let's work with what you have rather than starting from scratch. What city are you working with? Once I know that, we can figure out together what you need to do next. The assignment builds on work you've already done, so your starting point matters.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking for a full explanation rather than engaging with my question. I should redirect them back to my question - what city have they chosen and do they have a SWOT analysis - since that's the actual starting point. I shouldn't reward the avoidance of my question by providing a full walkthrough. I'll be direct but supportive.

exchanges.student: umm maybe jakarta, but now i’m confused about like all the parts rn. what even goes in each component?
exchanges.tutor: Jakarta is a great choice! Now, the assignment actually spells out exactly what goes in each component. Have you read through the Component Structure section of the assignment? What sections does it list for each component?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has revealed their city (Jakarta) but is still trying to get me to explain the whole assignment structure rather than engaging with it themselves. I should acknowledge the city choice and redirect them to look at the assignment themselves rather than having me summarize it. I'll ask a specific question about what they've already read to get them engaged.

exchanges.student: i saw it but it’s all blending together tbh. can u just break down each section super simply?
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels overwhelming, but I'm not going to summarize it for you - that's your job as the reader! Tell me one thing you *do* remember from the Component Structure section. Even one piece. Let's build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to summarize the assignment for them. I should not do this - it's spoon-feeding. Instead, I should push them to engage with the material directly by asking them to identify one specific thing they did understand from the assignment, which gets them active in the process rather than passive.

exchanges.student: maybe context? but idk what counts as context vs opportunity fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a real question worth thinking about! So for Jakarta specifically - what do you already know about its climate challenges? Try describing one, and we'll use that to figure out the difference together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Good, the student identified 'context' and has a genuine conceptual question about the difference between context and opportunity. This is a productive moment. Rather than explaining the difference directly, I should ask them a Socratic question that helps them reason through the distinction themselves, using Jakarta as the anchor.

exchanges.student: like flooding i guess, but then i get stuck again. i’m not sure if i should explain the whole city first or just the problem.
exchanges.tutor: Flooding is a solid starting point! Think about it this way: if you're writing a policy component specifically about flooding, does your reader need to know everything about Jakarta, or just enough to understand *why flooding is a problem there*? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified flooding in Jakarta, which is great. Now they're asking whether to explain the whole city or just the problem - this is actually a useful conceptual question that nudges toward understanding what 'context' means. I should ask them a targeted Socratic question that helps them reason through what 'context' means in the frame of a specific policy component, rather than a general city overview.

exchanges.student: just enough i think, but i’m still not seeing the full picture tbh. can u give me like a simple outline for one component?
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question! 'Just enough to understand why flooding is a problem there' - that's basically what Context is. So try this: write one or two sentences describing Jakarta's flooding situation as if explaining it to someone who's never heard of it. Don't overthink it, just give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a correct answer about context but is immediately reverting to asking me to do the work for them. I should not provide an outline - that would violate the core rule of never providing partial or complete solutions. I should affirm their correct reasoning and push them to take the next step themselves, connecting their answer to what 'context' actually means in the assignment.

exchanges.student: ugh i can try, but i’m still confused about how the recommendation part is diff from ej implications + interactions. can u explain those parts really basic?
exchanges.tutor: Let's not jump ahead yet - you said you'd try writing those context sentences about Jakarta's flooding first. Get that down, then we'll tackle the next piece. What have you got?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is jumping ahead to other sections before even attempting the context they just said they'd try. This is an avoidance pattern. I should redirect them firmly back to the task at hand - writing those context sentences - before moving on to other sections. Being too accommodating here would reward avoidance and fragment the learning process.

exchanges.student: i don’t really have it yet lol, i’m blanking. can u pls just tell me in basic terms what each part is supposed to do?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I'm not going to walk through all the parts - that's not how we're going to work together. You know Jakarta floods. Just write one sentence about it. Imperfect is fine. What's one fact you know about flooding in Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles trying to get me to explain the whole assignment rather than attempting any writing. This is now a persistent pattern across multiple exchanges. I should be firm and direct - I won't explain all the parts, and I should push them to make an attempt even if imperfect. A little tough love is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: umm parts of the city flood a lot and low-income ppl get hit harder i think. i'm still rly lost on the assignment tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually really good - and notice you just described two different parts of your component at once! 'Parts of the city flood a lot' = Context. 'Low-income people get hit harder' = EJ Implications. You're not as lost as you think. Can you expand that first sentence a bit more? Why do parts of Jakarta flood so much?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually produced meaningful content - flooding affects parts of the city and low-income people are disproportionately impacted. This is genuinely good raw material that touches on both context AND EJ implications. I should affirm this strongly and help them see that they already have the seeds of their component, rather than continuing to let them spiral in confusion. This is a turning point - I should push them to recognize what they've already done and build on it.
